2: the 24th letter of the Roman alphabet

1: An amido derivative of xanthic acid obtained as a whitecrystalline substance, C2H5O.CS.NH2; -- called also xanthogen amide.
a salt or ester of xanthic acid
xanthoma of the eyelids; occurs chiefly in the elderly
Of or pertaining to Xanthus, an ancient town on Asia Minor; --applied especially to certain marbles found near that place, and nowin the British Museum.
A compound or derivative of xanthogen. [Archaic]

A genus of minute unicellular algæ of the desmids. These alg¨ave a rounded shape and are armed with glochidiate or branchedaculei. Several species occur in ditches, and others are found fossilin flint or hornstone.

A white microcrystalline nitrogenous compound, C5H4O2N4,present in muscle tissue, in the liver, spleen, pancreas, and otherorgans, and also in urine (in small quantities) and some urinarycalculi, and in the juices of certain plants; -- so called because itleaves a yellow residue when evaporated t...
crystalline oxidation product of the metabolism of nucleoproteins; precursor of uric acid; found in many organs and in urine
A complex nitrogenous substance related to urea and uric acid,produced as a white powder; -- so called because it forms yellowsalts, and because its solution forms a blue fluorescence likequinine.
coarse herbs having small heads of greenish flowers followed by burrs with hooked bristles
A combining form from Gr. xanqo`s yellow; as in xanthocobalticsalts. Used also adjectively in chemistry.
A division of the Caucasian races, comprising the lighter-colored members.
Having a yellowish or fair complexion; of or pertaining to theXanthochroi.
Having a yellowish or fair complexion. -- n.
Abnormal coloration of feathers in which yellow replaces thenormal color, as in certain parrots. It is commonly due to lack ofthe dark pigment which with yellow forms green.
Producing a yellow color or compound; xanthic.  See Xanthicacid, under Xanthic.
a skin problem marked by the development (on the eyelids and neck and back) of irregular yellow nodules; sometimes attributable to disturbances of cholesterol metabolism
widespread xanthomas (especially on elbows and knees); often associated with a disorder of lipid metabolism
Of or pertaining to the lighter division of the Melanochroi, orthose races having an olive or yellow complexion and black hair.
a genus of bacteria similar to Pseudomonas but producing a yellow pigment that is not soluble in water
The yellow pigment present in the inner segments of the retinain animals.  See Chromophane.
yellow carotenoid pigments in plants and animal fats and egg yolks
Having a yellow stipe, or stem.
Pertaining to, or derived from, xanthoprotein; showing thecharacters of xanthoprotein; as, xanthoproteic acid; thexanthoproteic reaction for albumin.
A yellow acid substance formed by the action of hot nitric acidon albuminous or proteid matter. It is changed to a deep orange-yellow color by the addition of ammonia.
visual defect in which objects appear to have a yellowish hue; sometimes occurs in cases of jaundice
One of three alkaloids found in the root of the yellow puccoon(Hydrastis Canadensis). It is a yellow crystalline substance, andresembles berberine.
A glucoside extracted from Persian berries as a yellowcrystalline powder, used as a dyestuff.

A genus of shrubby ranunculaceous plants of North America,including only the species Xanthorhiza apiifolia, which has roots ofa deep yellow color; yellowroot. The bark is intensely bitter, and issometimes used as a tonic.
A genus of endogenous plants, native to Australia, having athick, sometimes arborescent, stem, and long grasslike leaves. SeeGrass tree.
An orange-yellow substance found in pigment spots of certaincrabs.
an abnormal yellow discoloration of the skin
Yellow; specifically (Ethnol.), of or pertaining to those racesof man which have yellowish, red, auburn, or brown hair.
A liquid hydrocarbon of the terpene series extracted from theseeds of a Japanese prickly ash (Xanthoxylum pipertium) as anaromatic oil.
A genus of prickly shrubs or small trees, the bark and rots ofwhich are of a deep yellow color; prickly ash.
Spanish missionary and Jesuit who establish missionaries in Japan and Ceylon and the East Indies (1506-1552)

A small three-masted vessel, with projecting bow stern andconvex decks, used in the Mediterranean for transporting merchandise,etc. It carries large square sails, or both. Xebecs were formerlyarmed and used by corsairs.
A Spartan institution which prohibited strangers from residingin Sparta without permission, its object probably being to preservethe national simplicity of manners.
alternative names for the family comprising the New Zealand wrens
A present given to a guest or stranger, or to a foreignambassador.

A house for the reception of strangers.(b) In the Middle Ages, a room in a monastery for the reception andentertainment of strangers and pilgrims, and for the relief ofpaupers. [Called also Xenodocheion.]
denoting or relating to cells or tissues from individuals belonging to different species
Of or pertaining to xenogenesis; as, the xenogenetic origin ofmicrozymes. Huxley.
(geology) a piece of rock of different origin from the igneous rock in which it is embedded
A mania for, or an inordinate attachment to, foreign customs,institutions, manners, fashions, etc. [R.] Saintsbury.
A suborder of soft-rayed fresh-water fishes of which theblackfish of Alaska (Dallia pectoralis) is the type.
a colorless odorless inert gaseous element occurring in the earth's atmosphere in trace amounts
a fear of foreigners or strangers
suffering from xenophobia; having abnormal fear or hatred of the strange or foreign
Greek general and historian; student of Socrates (430-355 BC)
in some classifications the family of the genus Xenopus which is otherwise included in the family Pipidae
A suborder of fishes including Gobiesox and allied genera.These fishes have soft-rayed fins, and a ventral sucker supported infront by the pectoral fins. They are destitute of scales.
an African clawed frog; in some classifications made the type genus of a separate family Xenopodidae
type and sole genus of Xenosauridae: slender-bodied Mexican lizards having the upper surface covered with tiny granules and tubercles
a brown-to-yellow mineral that is a phosphate of yttrium in crystalline form
The radical characteristic of xenylic compounds.
Pertaining to, derived from, designating, certain amidocompounds obtained by reducing certain nitro derivatives of diphenyl.
any plant of the genus Xeranthemum native to southern Europe having chaffy or silvery flower heads with purplish tubular flowers
An old money of account in Bombay, equal to three fifths of arupee.
A gold coin formerly current in Egypt and Turkey, of the valueof about 9s. 6d., or about $2.30; -- also, in Morocco, a ducat.
a mild form of ichthyosis characterized by abnormal dryness and roughness of the skin
forming an image by the action of light on a specially coated charged plate; the latent image is developed with powders that adhere only to electrically charged areas
edge enhancement is intrinsic in xerography

Pertaining to, or designating, an acid, C8H12O4, related tofumaric acid, and obtained from citraconic acid as an oily substancehaving a bittersweet taste; -- so called from its tendency to formits anhydride.
Among the primitive Christians, the living on a diet of dryfood in Lent and on other fasts.
Drought-loving; able withstand the absence or lack of moisture.Plants which are peculiarly adapted to dry climates are termed by DeCandolle xerophilous. Goodale.
abnormal dryness of the conjunctiva and cornea of the eyes; may be due to a systemic deficiency of vitamin A
small genus of North American herbs having grasslike basal leaves: squaw grass; sometimes placed in family Melanthiaceae
adapted to a xeric (or dry) environment
abnormal dryness of the mouth resulting from decreased secretion of saliva
a copy made by a xerographic printer
2: a duplicator (trade mark Xerox) that copies graphic matter by the action of light on an electrically charged photoconductive insulating surface in which the latent image is developed with a resinous powder

1: a member of the Negroid people of southern South Africa
2: a community of Negroid people in southern South Africa
3: a Bantu language closely related to Zulu

2: the 14th letter of the Greek alphabet

1: a city of central China; capital of ancient Chinese empire 221-206 BC
A genus of plants of the order Hæmodraceæ, having two-ranked,sword-shaped leaves.
Of, pertaining to, or resembling, a cetacean of the genusXiphius or family Xiphiidæ.
The posterior, or fourth, lateral plate in the plastron ofturtles; -- called also xiphisternum.
A genus of cetaceans having a long, pointed, bony beak, usuallytwo tusklike teeth in the lower jaw, but no teeth in the upper jaw.
An extinct genus of artiodactylous mammals found in theEuropean Tertiary formations. It had slender legs, didactylous feet,and small canine teeth.
Same as Limuloidea. Called also Xiphosura. X ray. See underRay.

The first two letters of the Greek word XRISTOS, Christ; -- anabbreviation used with the letters separate or, oftener, in amonogram, often inclosed in a circle, as a symbol or emblem ofChrist. It use as an emblem was introduced by Constantine the Great,whence it is known as the Constantinian symb...
A vacuum tube suitable for producing Röntgen rays.

2: (genetics) normal complement of sex chromosomes in a female
(genetics) abnormal complement of three X chromosomes in a female

1: An acid amide derivative of xylic acid, obtained as a whitecrystalline substance.
A gummy substance of the pentosan class, present in woodytissue, and yielding xylose on hydrolysis; wood gum.
Wood coal, or charcoal; -- so called in distinction frommineral coal.
type genus of Xylariaceae; fungi with perithecia in the upper part of erect black woody stromata
family of fungi characterized by dark brown to black spores
the woody part of plants: the supporting and water-conducting tissue, consisting primarily of tracheids and vessels
a colorless flammable volatile liquid hydrocarbon used as a solvent
Any one of six metameric phenol derivatives of xylene, obtainedas crystalline substances, (CH3)2.C6H3.OH.
Pertaining to, or designating, a complex acid related tomesitylenic acid, obtained as a white crystalline substance by theaction of sodium and carbon dioxide on crude xylenol.
Pertaining to, derived from, or related to, xylene;specifically, designating any one of several metameric acids producedby the partial oxidation of mesitylene and pseudo-cumene.
Pertaining to, or designating, either one of two distinct acidswhich are derived from xylic acid and related compounds, and aremetameric with uvitic acid.

Any one of six metameric hydrocarbons, (CH3)2.C6H3.NH2,resembling aniline, and related to xylene. They are liquids, oreasily fusible crystalline substances, of which three are derivedfrom metaxylene, two from orthoxylene, and one from paraxylene. Theyare called the amido xylenes.
A green or blue pigment produced by Peziza in certain kinds ofdecayed wood, as the beech, oak, birch, etc., and extracted as anamorphous powder resembling indigo.
A liquid hydrocarbon found in crude wood spirits.
A yellow oil having a geraniumlike odor, produced as a sideproduct in making phorone; -- called also xylite oil.
A combining form from Gr. xy`lon wood; as in xylogen,xylograph.
The dried twigs of a Syrian tree (Balsamodendron Gileadense).U. S. Disp.
Bearing fruit which becomes hard or woody.
An engraving on wood, or the impression from such an engraving;a print by xylography.
Resembling wood; having the nature of wood.
A substance resembling pyroxylin, obtained by the action ofnitric acid on starch; -- called also nitramidin.
The branch of dendrology treating of the gross and minutestructure of wood.
A genus of marine bivalves which bore holes in wood. They areallied to Pholas.
A tribe or family of dipterous flies whose larvæ live indecayed wood. Some of the tropical species are very large.
One of a tribe of beetles (Xylophili) whose larvæ live ondecayed wood.
Of or pertaining to the xylophilans.

An instrument common among the Russians, Poles, and Tartars,consisting of a series of strips of wood or glass graduated in lengthto the musical scale, resting on belts of straw, and struck with twosmall hammers. Called in Germany strohfiedel, or straw fiddle.
tropical evergreen trees or shrubs; chiefly African
Formed of wood pulp by molds; relating to casts made of woodpulp in molds.
The art or practice of burning pictures on wood with a hotiron; -- called also poker painting. See Poker picture, under Poker.
Any one of a group of quinone compounds obtained respectivelyby the oxidation of certain xylidine compounds. In general they areyellow crystalline substances.
A derivative of xylene obtained as a white crystallinesubstance which on exposure in the air becomes red; -- called alsobetaorcin.
An unfermentable sugar of the pentose class, C5H10O5, formed bythe hydrolysis of xylan; wood sugar.
shrub or small tree grown as an ornamental in mild climates for its neat evergreen foliage and fragrant late flowers; native of China
A glucoside found in the poisonous berries of a species ofhoneysuckle (Lonicera xylosteum), and extracted as a bitter, white,crystalline substance.
One versed or engaged in xylotomy.
Capable of boring or cutting wood; -- said of many insects.
Art of preparing sections (transverse, tangential, or radial)of wood, esp. by means of a microtome, for microscopic examination.
A genus of marine bivalves closely allied to Teredo, andequally destructive to timber. One species (Xylotrya fimbriata) isvery common on the Atlantic coast of the United States.
Any one of three metameric radicals which are characteristicrespectively of the three xylenes.
Any one of three metameric radicals, CH2.C6H4.CH2, derivedrespectively from the three xylenes. Often used adjectively; as,xylylene alcohol.
plants of tropical to temperate regions; usually in wet places
Of or pertaining to a natural order (Xyrideæ) of endogenousplants, of which Xyris is the type.
chiefly American marsh plants, having usually yellow flowers
